sessions.title: Training Partner Reality Check
sessions.principle:
Training partners are not opponents. They are responsibility.
The person across from you trusts you with their safety and their progress.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we protect our partners.
Responsibility comes before ego.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Answer honestly: What makes someone a bad training partner? What makes someone a good one? Which one are you today?
questions: What makes someone unsafe to train with?
What makes someone trustworthy to train with?
How does your behavior affect your partner’s progress?
Which type of partner are you right now?
What must change first?

scripture.text: As iron sharpens iron, so one person sharpens another.

sessions.title: Safety First
sessions.principle:
Safety is the foundation of trust in combat training.
Great rooms train hard but protect each other.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we train hard and protect bodies.
Respect keeps training alive.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Describe a moment when someone trained safely with you.
questions: What did that athlete do that made training feel safe?
Why did that matter to your trust?
What unsafe behavior damages trust fastest?
How can you protect someone better this week?

scripture.text: Do to others as you would have them do to you.

sessions.title: Training Honesty
sessions.principle:
Honest training creates honest improvement.
Going too easy wastes time. Going too hard breaks trust.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we train honestly.
Honesty builds skill.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Explain what honest training effort looks like.
questions: What is dishonest training?
Why do athletes fake effort?
What happens when intensity is too low?
What happens when intensity is too high?
What does honest effort require?

scripture.text: The integrity of the upright guides them.

sessions.title: Partner Responsibility
sessions.principle:
Training partners share responsibility for improvement.
Your partner’s progress partly depends on your discipline.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we help partners grow.
Responsibility grows skill.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Describe how you can help your partner improve during practice.
questions: What responsibility do partners have for each other’s growth?
How does careless drilling hurt both athletes?
What does an engaged partner do differently?
How can your focus help your partner learn better?

scripture.text: Carry each other’s burdens.

sessions.title: Protecting the Body
sessions.principle:
Bodies are tools for learning.
Injuries steal progress, and protecting partners protects the room.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we protect bodies.
Control prevents damage.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Describe how athletes can protect each other during training.
questions: Why do injuries happen in practice?
What warning signs should athletes watch for?
How does control prevent unnecessary damage?
What safety habits should never be skipped?

scripture.text: The prudent see danger and take refuge.

sessions.title: Partner Communication
sessions.principle:
Communication keeps training safe and productive.
Simple communication prevents confusion and injuries.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we communicate clearly.
Clear communication builds trust.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Describe how athletes should communicate during practice.
questions: Why is communication important during drills and live work?
What happens when athletes stay silent?
When should you speak up during training?
How does clear communication build trust?

scripture.text: Let your conversation be always full of grace.

sessions.title: Training Awareness
sessions.principle:
Awareness prevents accidents.
Good athletes stay aware of their surroundings and partners.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we stay aware.
Awareness prevents harm.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Explain how awareness protects your partner.
questions: Why is awareness important in partner work?
What causes avoidable accidents in training?
How do space and positioning matter?
What habits help you stay aware?

scripture.text: Be alert and sober minded.

sessions.title: Partner Trust
sessions.principle:
Trust makes intense training possible.
Athletes train harder when they trust their partners.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we earn trust.
Trust creates strong training.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Describe how athletes earn trust from partners.
questions: What behaviors build trust fastest?
What behaviors break trust fastest?
How does consistency affect trust?
What can you do to repair trust if it is damaged?

scripture.text: A faithful person can be trusted.

sessions.title: Listening Under Correction
sessions.principle:
Partners improve faster when they listen without ego.
If you resist feedback, you slow down both yourself and your partner.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we listen to learn.
Correction is a gift when pride gets out of the way.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How do you react when a partner or coach corrects you during drills? What should your response be instead?
questions: Why do athletes resist correction?
How does ego hurt training?
What response shows maturity?
What does listening well look like in action?

scripture.text: Whoever loves discipline loves knowledge.

sessions.title: Matching Drill Intensity
sessions.principle:
Good partners match the purpose of the drill, not their ego.
Too high wastes learning. Too low wastes reps.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we match the purpose of training.
The right intensity teaches.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Describe what happens when drill intensity is too high or too low. What does proper intensity look like?
questions: What happens when athletes overpower a drill?
What happens when they underwork it?
How do you know the purpose of the rep?
What does proper intensity require?
sessions.deliverable: too high + too low + proper standard

scripture.text: There is a time for every activity under the heavens.

sessions.title: Speaking With Respect
sessions.principle:
How you speak to a partner affects trust, effort, and learning.
Training hard does not mean speaking carelessly.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we speak with respect.
Your mouth can build the room or break it.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What kind of language helps training? What kind of language hurts it? Give one example of each.
questions: What kind of words build trust?
What kind of words damage it?
How should disciplined athletes speak under frustration?
What tone keeps correction helpful?

scripture.text: Let no corrupting talk come out of your mouths.

sessions.title: Tapping, Stopping, Respecting Limits
sessions.principle:
Respect means recognizing limits before damage happens.
Ignoring taps or warning signs is not toughness. It is immaturity.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we respect limits.
Real control protects people before damage starts.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Why is it important to recognize taps, injuries, and unsafe positions immediately? What does mature response look like?
questions: Why do some athletes ignore warning signs?
What does immaturity look like here?
What does mature control look like?
Why does stopping early protect the room?

scripture.text: A wise man has great power, and a man of knowledge increases strength.

sessions.title: Accepting Different Partners
sessions.principle:
Discipline means training well with different bodies, skill levels, and personalities.
Honor means adapting without complaining.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we train well with who is in front of us.
Adaptability is part of respect.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What makes some partners harder for you to work with? How should you respond instead of complaining or checking out?
questions: What kind of partners frustrate you most?
How do you usually respond?
What would maturity look like instead?
How can a difficult partner still help you grow?

scripture.text: Do everything without grumbling.

sessions.title: Being Present for the Drill
sessions.principle:
A distracted partner wastes both athletes’ reps.
Presence is part of respect.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we are present for the rep.
Presence is respect in action.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How do you know when you are mentally absent in drills? What does it cost your partner? What is your reset cue?
questions: What makes you mentally drift?
How does that affect your partner?
What sign tells you that you are absent?
What cue brings you back?
sessions.deliverable: absence sign + partner cost + reset cue

scripture.text: Whatever your hand finds to do, do it with your might.

sessions.title: Ego in the Room
sessions.principle:
Ego turns partners into targets instead of teachers.
When you need to prove yourself every round, you stop learning.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we train to improve, not to perform ego.
Ego wins rounds and loses growth.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How does ego show up during partner work? What does it make athletes do? What is the disciplined alternative?
questions: What does ego look like in training?
How does it hurt partners?
Why is proving yourself a trap?
What attitude replaces ego?

scripture.text: Do nothing out of selfish ambition or vain conceit.

sessions.title: Trust Retest Within Training
sessions.principle:
Trust is built in the small habits of daily training.
Trust is not claimed. It is earned through behavior.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, trust is earned daily.
Trust grows quietly and breaks quickly.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: List 3 habits that make you trustworthy in training and 2 habits that weaken trust. Which one must change now?
questions: What makes athletes trustworthy?
What weakens trust quickly?
What one habit matters most for you?
What change should happen immediately?

scripture.text: Whoever walks in integrity walks securely.

sessions.title: Do Not Waste Your Partner’s Time
sessions.principle:
Low effort does not only hurt you. It steals reps from your partner.
Effort is shared responsibility.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we do not waste reps.
A wasted rep hurts two people.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How can an athlete waste a partner’s time in practice? Which one do you need to fix most?
questions: What behaviors waste reps?
Why do athletes fall into them?
How does it hurt your partner?
Which behavior do you need to fix first?

scripture.text: Make the best use of the time.

sessions.title: Reliable Effort
sessions.principle:
Partners should not have to guess which version of you showed up today.
Reliability is part of honor.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, effort is reliable.
Consistency is a gift to your partner.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Is your training effort consistent or mood-based? Give one example. What should reliability look like instead?
questions: What makes effort inconsistent?
How does that affect partners?
What would reliable effort look like?
Where do you need to tighten up?

scripture.text: Let us not grow weary of doing good.

sessions.title: Showing Up Ready
sessions.principle:
Preparation is respect before the drill even starts.
Readiness is part of partnership.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we show up ready.
Preparation is respect before contact.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What habits show that you are ready to train with a partner? Which habit do you need to improve?
questions: What does readiness look like before practice starts?
What habits weaken readiness?
How does poor preparation affect partners?
What one habit must improve now?

scripture.text: Prepare your work outside.

sessions.title: Honest Resistance
sessions.principle:
Great partners give the right amount of resistance for real growth.
Too little creates fake confidence. Too much ruins learning.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we give honest resistance.
Honest resistance creates real skill.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What is the difference between fake resistance, reckless resistance, and honest resistance?
questions: What does fake resistance create?
What does reckless resistance create?
What makes resistance honest?
How do you match the goal of the rep?

scripture.text: Faithful are the wounds of a friend.

sessions.title: Energy in the Room
sessions.principle:
Your attitude affects your partner’s work whether you notice it or not.
Negative energy spreads. So does disciplined energy.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we bring working energy.
Energy is contagious. So is discipline.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What kind of energy do you usually bring into partner work? How does it affect the person across from you?
questions: What kind of energy weakens a rep?
What kind of energy strengthens it?
What do you usually bring?
How do partners feel your energy?

scripture.text: Encourage one another and build each other up.

sessions.title: Work Through Frustration
sessions.principle:
A disciplined partner does not punish others for personal frustration.
Control means frustration does not spill onto others.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, frustration does not control the rep.
Control means your frustration does not spill onto others.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What happens to your partner work when you get frustrated? What should controlled response look like instead?
questions: How does frustration change your training?
How does it affect partners?
What response shows maturity?
What should you do before the next rep?
sessions.deliverable: frustration pattern + partner cost + mature response

scripture.text: A person without self-control is like a city broken into.

sessions.title: Shared Improvement
sessions.principle:
Partner work is not a one-way benefit. Both athletes should grow.
The strongest rooms are built by shared improvement instead of private winning.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, both people improve.
Strong partners create shared gain.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How can you help a partner improve while still improving yourself?
questions: What makes partner work mutual?
What makes it selfish?
How do both athletes win the session?
What can you do to raise the level of the rep?

scripture.text: Look not only to your own interests, but also to the interests of others.

sessions.title: Personal Standard in Partner Work
sessions.principle:
You need a clear standard for how you train with others.
Standards make you trustworthy.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we bring a clear standard to the rep.
Clear standards create trustworthy partners.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Write your personal standard for partner work in one sentence.
questions: What should your partner always get from you?
What weak habits must disappear?
What standard will you hold?
Can someone else clearly see that standard?

scripture.text: Be steadfast in your work.

sessions.title: Train Hard, Never Reckless
sessions.principle:
Hard training and reckless training are not the same thing.
Strong rooms are built by disciplined intensity, not careless force.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we train hard, never reckless.
Discipline separates hard work from foolishness.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What is the difference between hard training and reckless training? Give one example of each.
questions: What makes training hard but disciplined?
What makes it reckless?
Why do athletes confuse the two?
How do you push hard without losing control?

scripture.text: The prudent see danger and take refuge.

sessions.title: Protecting Injured Partners
sessions.principle:
Respect means adjusting when a partner is injured, limited, or vulnerable.
Strong athletes do not exploit weakness in training for ego points.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we protect injured partners.
How you treat weakness reveals your strength.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How should an athlete adjust when a partner is hurt, limited, or returning from injury?
questions: What would immaturity look like here?
What would maturity look like?
Why does this matter to room trust?
How do you protect without treating someone as weak?

scripture.text: Carry each other’s burdens.

sessions.title: Control in Bad Positions
sessions.principle:
Control matters most when positions get messy, uncomfortable, or emotional.
Real control shows up when positions get ugly.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, control stays when positions get hard.
Real control shows up when positions get ugly.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What happens to your behavior in uncomfortable or chaotic positions? What should disciplined control look like instead?
questions: What usually breaks first under chaos?
How does panic affect control?
What restores discipline?
How do you protect your partner in bad positions?

scripture.text: Better a patient person than a warrior.

sessions.title: Respecting Beginner Partners
sessions.principle:
Beginners need protection, clarity, and discipline from experienced partners.
Protecting newer athletes is part of honor.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we protect beginners.
Your treatment of beginners reveals your true character.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How should you train with a newer or less skilled partner? What should never happen?
questions: How should experienced athletes treat beginners?
What behaviors damage trust fast?
What does mature partnership look like here?
How do you challenge without overwhelming?

scripture.text: Whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me.

sessions.title: Do Not Chase Cheap Wins
sessions.principle:
Cheap wins in practice often cost long-term trust and growth.
Some athletes chase little victories in the room instead of learning.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we do not chase cheap wins.
Cheap wins feed pride and starve growth.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What does a cheap win in practice look like? Why is it bad for both athletes?
questions: What behaviors count as cheap wins?
Why are they tempting?
What do they cost long term?
What mindset replaces them?
sessions.deliverable: cheap win definition + partner damage + better mindset

scripture.text: Pride goes before destruction.

sessions.title: Correcting Without Disrespect
sessions.principle:
Partners can help each other without talking down to each other.
Helpful correction does not need arrogance.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we correct with respect.
Helpful correction does not need arrogance.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: How can you help a partner without sounding arrogant, impatient, or disrespectful?
questions: What makes correction disrespectful?
What makes it helpful?
How should maturity sound?
When should correction be brief instead of a lecture?

scripture.text: Let your gentleness be evident to all.

sessions.title: Shared Safety Is Shared Responsibility
sessions.principle:
Safety is not one person’s job. Both partners carry it.
One mature athlete helps. Two mature athletes build a room.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, both partners own safety.
Shared safety creates shared trust.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What responsibilities belong to both partners in keeping training safe and productive?
questions: What is your part?
What is your partner’s part?
What happens when one athlete stops caring?
How does shared safety strengthen trust?

scripture.text: Two are better than one.

sessions.title: Respect Is Visible
sessions.principle:
Respect is not a private feeling. It is visible in behavior.
The truth shows in your pace, tone, control, and consistency.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, respect is visible.
Respect can be seen before it is spoken.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: List 5 behaviors that prove respect for a training partner.
questions: What does real respect look like?
What behavior proves it?
What behavior exposes fake respect?
What do you show right now?

scripture.text: By their fruit you will recognize them.

sessions.title: Make Your Partner Better
sessions.principle:
A great training partner does more than avoid harm. He helps the other person improve.
The best partners are builders, not just participants.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we make partners better.
The best partners are builders, not just participants.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What are 3 ways you can actively help a partner improve during practice?
questions: What helps partners improve fastest?
What weakens their learning?
How can you raise the level of the rep?
What does building look like in practice?

scripture.text: Encourage one another and build each other up.

sessions.title: Giving the Right Look
sessions.principle:
Partners help each other most when they give realistic looks without sabotaging learning.
The right look sharpens both athletes.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we give the right look.
The right look sharpens both athletes.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What does it mean to give a partner the right look in drills or live work?
questions: What makes a look useful?
What makes it fake?
What makes it reckless?
How do you match realism to the learning goal?
sessions.deliverable: useful look + fake look + reckless look

scripture.text: Iron sharpens iron.

sessions.title: Helping Without Carrying
sessions.principle:
Good partners help growth, but they do not do the work for the other athlete.
Real help still requires real work.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we help without carrying.
Real help still requires real work.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What is the difference between helping a partner and carrying a partner?
questions: When does helping become carrying?
Why is carrying harmful?
What is the healthier standard?
How do you support without faking success?

scripture.text: Each one should carry their own load.

sessions.title: Partners Under Pressure
sessions.principle:
Pressure reveals what kind of partner you really are.
It is easy to be respectful when everything is smooth. The truth comes out when you are tired, frustrated, losing, or embarrassed.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, pressure does not change our character.
Pressure reveals partner character.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What kind of partner do you become under pressure? What changes first: tone, effort, control, or attitude?
questions: What changes in you under pressure?
How does that affect partners?
What standard must stay fixed?
What usually breaks first?
sessions.deliverable: pressure pattern + partner effect + fixed standard

scripture.text: When you are tested, your faith produces perseverance.

sessions.title: Trustworthy in Live Work
sessions.principle:
Live work demands the highest level of trust, control, and honesty.
Your partner needs to know you will compete hard without losing discipline.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, live work stays disciplined.
Real trust survives real pace.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What makes someone trustworthy in live work? What makes someone dangerous in the wrong way?
questions: What builds trust in live rounds?
What destroys it?
What kind of live partner are you?
How do hard pace and control stay together?

scripture.text: Whoever can be trusted with very little can also be trusted with much.

sessions.title: Partner Reputation
sessions.principle:
Every athlete builds a reputation in the room as a partner.
Your reputation is built rep by rep.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, reputation is earned by behavior.
Your partner reputation is written by your habits.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: What reputation do you think you have as a training partner? What reputation do you want to earn instead?
questions: What reputation do your habits build?
What do others likely trust you for?
What must change?
What reputation do you want people to feel from you?

scripture.text: A good name is more desirable than great riches.

sessions.title: What Does Great Partnership Look Like?
sessions.principle:
Great partnership is disciplined, safe, honest, useful, and trustworthy.
The best partners in the room are the ones people trust to make training real, sharp, and safe.
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, great partners strengthen the room.
Great partnership is visible in action.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Define great training partnership in actions, not feelings. List 5 behaviors you will live out next week.
questions: What does great partnership look like?
What behavior proves it?
What weak habits must disappear?
What would someone else see if you really lived this out?

scripture.text: Let us consider how we may spur one another on.

sessions.title: Retest: Training Partner Reality Check
sessions.principle:
Growth in partnership should be tested, not assumed.
The question is simple: are you a more trustworthy, disciplined, useful partner now than when you started?
sessions.houseStandard:
In this house, we measure growth honestly.
Partnership growth is visible when honesty stays consistent.
Say it out loud. Twice.
sessions.prompt: Repeat Session 1 exactly. Answer again: What makes someone a bad training partner? What makes someone a good one? Which one are you today? Then compare your old answer to your new one.
questions: What changed most in how you train with partners?
What weakness still remains?
What standard improved most?
What is your next-step commitment?

scripture.text: Examine yourselves.
